speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October the 5th, 1995. Survivor is Stephanie Druc, nee Reich. Interviewer is Susi Coleman. This is Sydney, Australia. The language is English. And this is tape one.

speaker: interviewer
text: My is Susi Coleman. I'm interviewing survivor Stephanie Druc on the 5th of October, 1995, in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Not ?] [? Syndey. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give us your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stephanie [? Marilyn ?] Druc.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. What were those names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was [? Bulgatzka, ?] [? Stephania ?] [? Bulgatzka. ?] And later on, it was Stephanie [? Apechen. ?] Then it was Stephania [? Drutz, ?] written with a T-Z on the end, German way of spelling Druc, and then finally, the real one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And what was your date of birth, please, Stephanie?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany. Thank you very much. Stephanie, did you remain in Berlin after you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Born. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps a week or two weeks, I don't know how long my mother was in hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go then?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a small town called Krotoszyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that town, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Province Posen, and it's German. It was really Polish, part of Poland, but occupied by Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the reason that you were born in Berlin and not in this village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my mother went for the birth of the child to a bigger town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your family do in this village?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small town, not a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Town, sorry. Town, town.

speaker: interviewee
text: My fa-- at the time when I've been born, my father was in the Prussian army. And my mother was home duties.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they have relatives in this town, in this town, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The parents of my father lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Krotoszyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And what was, what was the Jewish population of this town? Do you know? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: There have been a few families, but I don't know exactly how many.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father return from the army? And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he went back to his occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was flour milling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he a property-- did he own his own mills? Or did he work for somebody else? Was he self-employed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my grandparents, my father's parents, have been grain merchants. And that gave my father the idea of buying the grain in the vicinity of this town but milling it across a border in Poland occupied by Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his motive for doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because grain was cheaper in Prussia but dearer the flour in Russia. And then he exported it to Russia proper also.

speaker: interviewer
text: As flour?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it a prosperous business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of a home did you live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember my home in Krotoszyn, because I've been only very young baby, a few months old. I've been born in March '18. In November '18, the war finished, ended. And my father opted. They had the right of option either to live permanently in Germany or in Poland. Since his business was in Poland, and since he was married to my mother, who was also from the occupied Polish town-- occupied by Russian-- you know, they moved to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the status of your parents' citizenship, was it Polish? Or was it German?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean by status? Citizenship or what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, what citizenship? Were they considered Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, once they opted for Poland, they became Polish citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about you being born in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that also included--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandma. I loved my grandma. She came with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was your nanny?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ida.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ida. And she was a German woman, was she? Or Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was German by origin, but Polish by name, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was not a Jewish woman. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. But with your being born in Berlin, did you, was your, did you take your father's citizenship or did you keep your own? What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got the Polish citizenship, because my father became a Polish citizen, as well the children. At this time, all the children got-- in Poland, at least-- got the citizenship of their father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your father conduct his business from Warsaw? Did he keep the mills, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not from Warsaw. Kalisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kalisz, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was across the border from Prussia into Russia. The first town was Kalisz. And if you know a bit of history, Kalisz was run over and burned down twice by the Germans in first World War and then in the second one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they moved to Kalisz, not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he still ran his business from Kalisz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The business of flour mill was in Kalisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the outskirts of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how long did you remain in Kalisz? Your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did remain in Kalisz till I finished the high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you have some brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a brother and a sister, which are twins, and then older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vera Felicya is my older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roman Sylvim is one of the twins, and Eva Friderika is the twin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Kalisz, was there a larger Jewish community, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a fairly large Hasidic Jewish community and a very small non-orthodox. As a matter of fact, when my father wanted to buy an organ for the non-orthodox synagogue, they objected to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who objected to it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hasidics.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so your father attended a non-orthodox shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: This synagogue was called the German Synagogue. But in fact, it wasn't German at all. The services had been conducted in Polish and a bit in Hebrew, not, nothing in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the intera--

speaker: interviewee
text: But because the reformed extremists, you probably know, came from Germany, this non-orthodox-- very much non-orthodox-- synagogue was called by the remainder of the Jews the German Synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Hasidic community in Kalisz. Was that a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very large.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes? Was that large? And was it indigenous to Kalisz? And was maybe--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The reformed people, people who come from this movement of peoples?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The Hasidics have been there for many, many centuries, because Kalisz is one of the oldest Polish little, small towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember anything of Jewish life there, either with the Hasidic community? Or was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: High holidays? Any celebration of things like this? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I do remember, yes. We, at my home, usually for the new year, there has been a meal for the family, especially [? might. ?] And father used to go to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there very much interaction between the two Jewish communities?

speaker: interviewee
text: None whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: None. Gosh. And was-- there was, there was a yeshiva, obviously, in Kalisz, [? and I would think there ?] for the city community.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't now. I never heard the word yeshiva before, only after I came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hasidic Jews in Kalisz, they had mostly tiny, little shops in one of two streets close by each other. And when I was in the shop was half a dozen kids hanging around there. And the husband has been in the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was, and what was your mother doing? What was she-- what was her--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was reading.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And playing the piano and being [? the lady. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember your home in Kalisz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe us? Can you describe it for--

words.text: us? Can you describe it for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, look, I've been what, 16 and 1/2, 17 when I left Kalisz. A 17-year-old girl remembers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like, your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a beautiful villa of 10 rooms and a garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have help in the house besides your nanny, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Yeah. There was a cook, [? Agnieszka-- ?] Agnes in English-- who believed that when she dies, she will be an angel in heaven with Jesus. Then there was Maria. That was the house maid for cleaning. Then there was a nurse, a sister-- nursing sister for the two little twins. And then there was my nanny, or relate as the adopted grandma. And father had his chauffeur and his-- what do you call it-- coachman. That's what.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you take holidays away from Kalisz? Where was the family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Every school holidays, we went to-- we went abroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Went abroad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: To where, for example?

speaker: interviewee
text: Switzerland, and Poland, and Czechoslovakia, in Europe-- mostly in Germany, really. Because the rest of my father's family was in Germany. He was the only one who opted for Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of the family was in Berlin, and part was, was it, as it was called then, Breslau. Today it is called Wroclaw. Today, it's Polish again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did any of those people survive the war, Stephanie?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of my father's cousin survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the approximate number of the family outside of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father's family, there have been five boys and a girl. The girl, my auntie, by the name of Martha, she migrated just before the war to Turkey. And she survived. And all the boys have been migrating to Australia also before the war. Some of them, not all, survived. And my father's oldest boys stayed in Germany with, with the parents. He didn't migrate. And then he got married and stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the relationship-- oh, and your mother, sorry, your mother's family was in Turek? Turek? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Came from Turek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Turek, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But lived in Kalisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they lived in Kalisz as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her, what did her family consistent of?

speaker: interviewee
text: One more sister. There had been two sisters, Maria and Sophia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to her sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that is a sad story, because at the, in the first World War, this Maria, with her husband by the name of Brauda-- you might know here the [? Brauderson ?] in Sydney-- they fled before the Germans into Russia. And in Moscow, the husband of Maria, of my auntie, got sick. He died there, but there was born a boy, Alexander. And because he was born in Russia, in Moscow, then in the second World War, he was called to the army. And especially being a doctor, the Russian army was after him. But the auntie and her daughter, Julia, they got, they didn't get as far in the second World War as in the first one. They also fled the Germans, but they fled through Ukraine. And the Germans caught up with them. And also, they Have not been anymore Jewish. Because they had to-- since the husband was sick in Moscow in the first World War, and Jews--

speaker: interviewer
text: The thing fell. Sorry, your microphone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. This one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, it fell.

speaker: interviewee
text: I put it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it's OK. We will put it back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where do you want me to start from?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no, it's OK. It's just from the first war. You were saying that she fled into the Ukraine. Hang on. Just wait till he gives me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the first--

speaker: interviewer
text: Shh, shh. Hang on just a minute, cause the tape's not rolling. Just one moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you stop for a moment, because-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were saying that she actually went into the Ukraine and was caught by the Germans there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in the first World War, Jews have not been allowed to stay in Moscow. And since her husband was sick and she had the baby boy, they had to become Christian. And in the second World War, she again fled to German. But this time, not toward Moscow, but towards Ukraine. And the Germans caught up with them. And because they have been Jewish by birth, the auntie and the daughter of her-- Maria and the daughter, Julia-- have been hanged in the square of a small town as a warning to other Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, dear. Yes. And so, from your mother's side--

speaker: interviewee
text: But the boy, Alex, he was already taken into the Russian Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived. He survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is he now? Is he still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after the war, he found us in [NON-ENGLISH]. And my adopted grandma gave him all sorts of things, because he had a wife and a baby. But I didn't-- I hadn't been at home when he came, although I know that he survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, and your grandparents at the beginning of the war, were any of them alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't had any more parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Grandparents. Nana?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandparents?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not parents. Yes. Were any of them alive at the beginning of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. No. No. And after-- so your family was in Kalisz. And your father was running his business. And you went to school. What types of schools did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Polish government gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but did you go to primary school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I haven't been in any primary school. It was private tuition for two years and then straight into the first year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Straight into, into primary school. And the primary school was government-run, is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just told you. I haven't been in any primary school.

speaker: interviewer
text: No primary school at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Home tuition for a couple of years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then straight to the gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: And straight to the gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you were in the gymnasium from what age?

speaker: interviewee
text: From first-- I've been nine-- to year eight, when I've been six and 1/2, 16 and 1/2, nearly 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I ask you why you would not sent to school? Was that common practice?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents believed that two, the first two years can be taught at home, and especially since the home was a bilingual one. We've been taught both languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which languages did you speak at home? Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: German and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, German and Polish. And when you graduated from-- when you were at the, sorry, when you at the gymnasium, did you, do you remember in that period what the relations were like between the Jewish community of Kalisz and the non-Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. There have been very few Jewish girls in the gymna-- it was an all-girl school, of course. There have been very few Jews. Because as you probably know, the Polish government didn't like to have in the government schools many Jews. There was a Jewish gymnasium in Kalisz, too. And most of the Jewish children, if they ever came to the gymnasium, they went to the Jewish gymnasium. Because they have not gained the entrances to the government one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that by numerus clausus also? Or was it a test, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't numerus clausus. It was a test. And that was the way of not letting them in. But there always has been a few who have been so good that they had to be accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many Jewish girls were at the gymnasium with you? Can you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: There has usually been one or two in each of the classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any anti-Semitic activity with your other students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. Not generally, but now and again, you could feel it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with the teachers? Was there any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not officially.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the reason that your parents did not send you to the Jewish gymnasium? Was it simply because you were [? a brat ?] and they  didn't want to send you, or was there another reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know how to answer this question.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, for the first education after the middle, after gymnasium-- or as you call it today, high school-- the universities. If you came from a state school, you had a much easier entrance. From the Jewish gymnasium, it was much harder to get into university. That's number one. Number two, the Jewish gymnasium taught Hebrew and usually religion and all this subject, where in the Polish gymnasium, you haven't had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what the relationship was like with the general community and the Jewish community in Kalisz in those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you remember an incident, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the Hasidic community, they used to wear these long, black frocks and the little--

speaker: interviewer
text: Payot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Curls or whatever you call it. And they stood out from the rest of the [? people. ?] And especially if they did well. Some of them, not all, some had been very, very poor. But there have been a few successful. And that always made for jealousy. But there have been also quite good relationships. I remember one of my, my uncle, [INAUDIBLE] he was a president of the town, though that was not anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father take part in any of the Jewish community? Did he hold any office in the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. He went once or twice a year to the synagogue. That was all. But he had some friends who would have been Jews, because Kalisz was a spot for the flour mills. And there has been one or two-- at least two which I remember-- as a Jewish flour millers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Obviously, your father must have had international connections at this time with his business. Did you hear him discuss or did you discuss at any point the rise of Nazism in Germany and how that might affect--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, first of all, had an another mill near Berlin and another one in Danzig, which is Gdansk today. And he read all the German papers. And I remember him saying, das [? wort ?] Hitler [? heisst ?] [? krieg-- ?] the word Hitler means war, though he knew exactly what's happening-- what was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he make any preparation or did he think of any way that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. You see, my father lost his great fortune, stopped being the millionaire fairly early, because he could not look after the three mills in different-- oh, and there was another rice mill as well-- in the different places. He couldn't be in all places at the same time, though he hired some people and they let him down. And with other words, he lost his fortune. And I've been only young, about what? 12 or 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in the early '30s.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to work. Apart from going to school, I've [INAUDIBLE]. And once he lost his fortune, he reverted to grain merchant, because that was where he came from, from his parents. And as long as he was alive, he kept his family in this way. But my father died in '38, and there have been still the twins at home, 10 years old, still young children. And my mother, who has never worked in her life and wasn't used to this change of lifestyle, she couldn't really take it, and a few months later committed suicide in '39-- January, '39. And there have been the two little children left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you and Vera as a result of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this, at that time, Vera was already married and in Warsaw. And I've been in Warsaw, because I studied at the university.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'd already started.

speaker: interviewee
text: And our grandmother adopted one. That's a [INAUDIBLE]. Looked after the two kids at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family financially support itself once your parents were not around?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my parents died, then the two twins-- we wanted them to finish the school year still at the schools in Kalisz. They went to a Jewish-- each to another Jewish family who kept them till the end of the school year. And then we brought them over to Warsaw. And they lived with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were living with Vera and her husband--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And study-- and were you living in an apartment in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Vera's husband was the manager of the Jewish students' hostel, boys hostel. And I've been the only girl student in the hostel. They often thanked me. They are 100. She is one. There have been 100 boys, and I've been the only girl. Anyway, we brought over the two kids and Ida into Warsaw. That was at the end of the school year. And not to very long afterwards, the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had gone to Warsaw in which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went at, in '35.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you studying at the university?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anthropogeography.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was under numerus clausus, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many Jewish students do you remember being in your faculty or your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, myself and another girl. But my parents and my sister and her husband, they wanted me to study dentistry, because that was regarded as a good profession for the future. And I didn't want it. And in the [? systematology ?] academy, there was an entrance exam. And I wrote that.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was, among the questions, there was, why do you want to be a dentist? And I wrote down, because my family forces me. And on the board later was the result of the exams. There was, next to my name, a sentence that say, don't support forced labor. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got my way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Stephanie, in the years-- this is '35, '36, '37, something like this, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the student body? Was there any political activity--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: About the rise of Nazism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was happening with the student body at that time? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very much. There has been a ripe anti-Semitism at the university. First of all, our indexes-- that is, books where all the professors had to sign that you made it-- have been stamped in different colors.

speaker: interviewer
text: For Jewish students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Green, green was for the non-Jews. And lilac stamps have been for the Jewish students, so that if you gave your index to the teacher or to the professor to--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your record of passing exams?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He immediately knew that you were a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did that affect your results, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not my results, but in many cases, it did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you see any violence at all at the university in this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I've seen girls thrown out of the window from the first floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was early '39. I also have been during the so-called student strike, where the Jewish students have striked and didn't go to university at all. They stayed in this Jewish hostel.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was managed by your brotherr-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And Jewish businesses, like bakeries and butchers, they sent into the house-- into this academic house, into the hostel-- food for the striking students.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the result of the strike?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get involved in any political organizations yourself-- Jewish poli-- on campus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. No, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: Vera's husband was a revisionist and very active. But I haven't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think you got politicized at all in that period? Personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. You see, I've been a full-time student. And I worked at the same time as a teller in a bank, because there have been two shifts. The late evening/night shift, I've been a teller. And at the same time, I have been studying full-time. And being Jewish on top of it, you had to work really hard. So there was not much time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was there a lot of discussion, obviously, if your brother-in-law was a revisionist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes, because being in this Jewish hostel with the students-- you know what it is with students, as always.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they say thought, they thought was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was quite evident that a war was coming. I remember a demonstration in front of the English embassy in Warsaw when they declared that they support-- they come to aid of Poland. There was a big demonstration in front of the English embassy.

speaker: interviewer
text: For or against the aid?

speaker: interviewee
text: For, because England has-- you know, they've been supporting the Poles against the Germans. They declared war to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Thank you, Stephanie. I think we'll stop there. Thank you.
